2026-03-02

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

We define the Catchment Connectivity Index \(CCI\) as the weighted sum of all dispersal probabilities between river reaches in our catchment.

\[CCI = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}{ I_{ij}\frac{w_{i}w_{j}}{W^2}}\] where:

  • \(I_{ij}\) is the dispersal probability between river reaches \(i\) and \(j\);
  • \(w_i\) and \(w_j\) are the weights of the river reaches (e.g. their length, or their discharge, or available habitat);
  • \(W\) is the total weight of the river network (e.g. total length, or total discharge, or available habitat).

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

The term \(I_{ij}\) is the dispersal probability between river reaches \(i\) and \(j\). We factorize it to express the effect of barriers and dispersal:

\[I_{ij} = B_{ij}c_{ij}\] where:

  • \(c_{ij}\) represents the effect of barriers when moving/dispersing from \(i\) to \(j\);
  • \(B_{ij}\) represents the effect of distance when moving from \(i\) to \(j\).

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

The term \(c_{ij}\) is the effect of barriers when moving/dispersing from \(i\) to \(j\). We express it as:

\[c_{ij} = \prod_{m = 1}^{k}{p_{m}^{eq}}\] where:

  • \(p_{m}^{eq}\) is the probability of crossing barrier \(m\) on the path between \(i\) and \(j\);
  • \(k\) is the number of barriers on the path between \(i\) and \(j\).

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

The term \(p_{m}^{eq}\) is a metric that considers the passability of barrier \(m\). We consider both cases when the dispersal is symmetric and when it is asymmetric.

For symmetric dispersal, we express \(p_{m}^{eq}\) as the product of upstream and downstream passabilities (that are measured values, e.g. from telemetry studies:

\[p_{m}^{eq} = p_{m}^{u}p_{m}^{d}\] If the directionality is important, we express \(p_{m}^{eq}\) as the passability in the direction of interest:

\[p_{m}^{eq} = \begin{cases} p_{m}^{u} & \text{if m is crossed while moving upstream in the i to j path}\\ p_{m}^{d} & \text{if m is crossed while moving downstream in the i to j path } x < 0 \end{cases} \]

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

The term \(B_{ij}\) considers the dispersal/movement of organisms across the river network. We can formulate the dispersal kernel as a function of the distance between river reaches \(i\) and \(j\):

\[ B_{ij} = PD^{d_{ij}} \]

where:

  • \(PD\) is the probability of dispersal per unit of distance (e.g. per 10s of km)
  • \(d_{ij}\) is the distance between river reaches \(i\) and \(j\) (e.g. in 10s km)

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

The term \(B_{ij}\) considers the dispersal/movement of organisms across the river network. Asymmeric dispersal can also be accounted for:

\[ B_{ij} = PD_{u}^{d_{ij}^{u}} PD_{d}^{d_{ij}^{d}} \]

where:

  • \(PD_{u}\) and \(PD_{d}\) are the upstream and downlstream probabilities of dispersal per unit of distance (e.g. per 10s of km)
  • \(d_{ij}^{u}\) and \(d_{ij}^{d}\) are the upstream/downstream travelled distances between river reaches \(i\) and \(j\) (e.g. in 10s km)

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

An alternative formulation for \(B_{ij}\) can be done with a step function:

\[B_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{when } d_{ij} < TR\\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

\[B_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{when } d_{ij}^{u} < TR_{u} \text{ and } d_{ij}^{d} < TR_{d} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \]

where:

  • \(TR\), \(TR_{u}\), \(TR_{d}\) are the threshold distances

Note that other dispersal kernels can be used (e.g. leptokurtic dispersal is well parametrized for many species and implemented in riverconn).

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

We define the Reach Connectivity Index \(RCI_{i}\) as the weighted sum of all dispersal probabilities entering or exiting reach \(i\).

\[RCI = \sum_{j=1}^{n}{ I_{ij}\frac{w_{j}}{W}}\] The index can be calculated considering:

  • only ‘inwards’ connections
  • only ‘outwards’ connections

Note that the Dendritic Connectivity Index for diadromous \(DCId\) fish can be calculated as \(RCI\) with the outlet as focal reach.

Connectivity/fragmentation indices

Barriers are prioritized using a leave-one-out approach. The importance of barrier \(m\) is calculated as the difference between the connectivity of the catchment with and without barrier \(m\):

\[\dCCI_{m} = \frac{CCI - CCI_{-m}}{CCI} 100\] where:

  • \(CCI\) is the connectivity of the catchment with all barriers;
  • \(CCI_{-m}\) is the connectivity of the catchment without barrier \(m\)

Which packages we will use

  • igraph for graph manipulation: useful for building/editing/managing graphs;

  • riverconn for calculating connectivity indices: the package we will use to calculate connectivity indices and prioritize barriers;

  • sf and terra for vector and raster data manipulation;

  • dplyr for data manipulation: helps building pipelines for easy-to-read data manipulation (loaded with the tidyverse);

  • ggplot2 for data visualization: a bit painful, but easily integrates sf and terra objects for plotting (loaded with the tidyverse).

Which packages we will use

library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(terra)
library(tidyterra)
library(viridis)
library(igraph)
library(riverconn)

Data preprocessing

Data preprocessing

We keep using the Ebro as a case study. we load the igraph object and the river shapefile we created in the previous lecture. We also identify the outlet.

river_graph <- read_rds('data/river_graph/river_graph.rds')

river_net_simplified <- st_read("data/river_graph/river_net_simplified.shp") %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(name = as.character(NodeID))
## Reading layer `river_net_simplified' from data source 
##   `D:\riverconn_course\data\river_graph\river_net_simplified.shp' 
##   using driver `ESRI Shapefile'
## Simple feature collection with 646 features and 17 fields
## Geometry type: LINESTRING
## Dimension:     XY
## Bounding box:  xmin: -4.277083 ymin: 40.41875 xmax: 1.997917 ymax: 43.11458
## Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84
outlet <- river_net_simplified$NodeID[river_net_simplified$DIST_DN_KM == 0 ]

Data preprocessings

river_graph
## IGRAPH f08f63a DN-- 646 645 -- 
## + attr: name (v/n), length (v/n), HYRIV_ID (v/n), NEXT_DOWN (v/n),
## | MAIN_RIV (v/n), LENGTH_KM (v/n), DIST_DN_KM (v/n), DIST_UP_KM (v/n),
## | CATCH_SKM (v/n), UPLAND_SKM (v/n), ENDORHEIC (v/n), DIS_AV_CMS (v/n),
## | ORD_STRA (v/n), ORD_CLAS (v/n), ORD_FLOW (v/n), HYBAS_L12 (v/n),
## | elevation (v/n), type (e/c), id_links (e/n), id_barrier (e/c), pass_u
## | (e/n), pass_d (e/n)
## + edges from f08f63a (vertex names):
##  [1] 515->516 520->516 519->520 496->520 276->160 153->160 160->164 162->161
##  [9] 161->164 642->640 646->640 641->643 645->643 638->635 640->635 636->629
## [17] 644->629 632->631 639->631 629->628 631->630 630->628 628->627 627->624
## + ... omitted several edges
V(river_graph)$name <- as.character(V(river_graph)$name)
river_graph <- delete_vertex_attr(river_graph, "LENGTH_KM")
V(river_graph)$length = V(river_graph)$length/10000

Data preprocessing

  • suit_fun_high(): habitat suitability for high-altitude organisms
  • suit_fun_low(): habitat suitability for low-altitude organisms
  • we use these functions to assign a habitat suitability index (HSI) to each node of the graph.
suit_fun_high <- function(x){dnorm(x, mean = 1500, sd = 500)*410*sqrt(3*pi)}
suit_fun_low <- function(x){exp(- 0.001*x)}

V(river_graph)$HSI_low <- suit_fun_low(V(river_graph)$elevation)
V(river_graph)$HSI_high <- suit_fun_high(V(river_graph)$elevation)

Data preprocessing

A quick check on how the habitat suitability indices look like.

plot <- 1:10:2500 %>%
  data.frame("Elevation" = .,
             "Low" = suit_fun_low(.), 
             "High" = suit_fun_high(.)) %>%
  pivot_longer(c("Low", "High"), 
               names_to = "Type", values_to = "HSI") %>%
  ggplot() + 
  geom_line(aes(x = Elevation, y = HSI, color = Type))+
  theme_bw() + xlab("Elevation (m)") + ylab("Habitat Suitability Index (HSI)")

Data preprocessing

plot

CCI with riverconn

CCI with riverconn

We calculate the Carchment Connectivity Index and then play around with the function parameters.

  • We set B_ij_flag = FALSE because we do not have a dispersal kernel in this example.
  • We set index_type = "full" because we are interested in calculating the \(CCI\).
  • We set the weight to the field “length”.
g <- river_graph
index_calculation(graph = g, 
                  weight = "length",
                  B_ij_flag = FALSE, 
                  index_type = "full")$index
## [1] 0.09043146

CCI with riverconn

Now we calculate the same index, but using asymmetric dispersal.

  • The index is higher because pass_u = 0.1 and pass_d = 0.8, while for the product is always used.
  • Note that some arguments are omitted because the default values are used (see documentation).
g <- river_graph
index_calculation(g, dir_fragmentation_type = "asymmetric", B_ij_flag = FALSE )$index
## [1] 0.2510495

CCI with riverconn

Now we edit the passabilities.

  • We set the upstream passability to 0.5: \(CCI\) increases.
  • We set the upstream passability to 0.1: \(CCI\) decreases.
g <- river_graph
E(g)$pass_u <- E(g)$pass_d  <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0.5,NA)
index_calculation(g, B_ij_flag = FALSE)$index
## [1] 0.1260329
E(g)$pass_u <- E(g)$pass_d  <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0,NA)
index_calculation(g, B_ij_flag = FALSE)$index
## [1] 0.07812939

CCI with riverconn

We check the effect of the passability on the asymmetric indices.

  • We set the upstream passability to 0.5: \(CCI\) increases.
  • We set the upstream passability to 0.1: \(CCI\) decreases.
g <- river_graph
E(g)$pass_u <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0.5,NA)
index_calculation(g, dir_fragmentation_type = "asymmetric",  B_ij_flag = FALSE)$index
## [1] 0.3417609
E(g)$pass_u <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0,NA)
index_calculation(g, dir_fragmentation_type = "asymmetric",  B_ij_flag = FALSE)$index
## [1] 0.2379491

CCI with riverconn

We include the dispersal limitation with an exponentially decaying dispersal kernel

  • We set the \(PD\) parameter to 0.8.
  • We set the \(PD\) parameter to 0.3.
g <- river_graph
index_calculation(g, disp_type = "exponential", param = 0.8)$index
## [1] 0.01371438
index_calculation(g, disp_type = "exponential", param = 0.3)$index
## [1] 0.003304613

CCI with riverconn

We include an asymmetric dispersal (dir_distance_type = "asymmetric"), we change the upstream dispersal probability.

  • We set the \(PD_{u}\) parameter to 0.8.
  • We set the \(PD_{u}\) parameter to 0.3: lower dispersal upstream, lower \(CCI\).
g <- river_graph
index_calculation(g, disp_type = "exponential", dir_distance_type = "asymmetric", 
                  param_u = 0.8, param_d = 0.5)$index
## [1] 0.007458711
index_calculation(g, disp_type = "exponential", dir_distance_type = "asymmetric", 
                  param_u = 0.8, param_d = 0.2)$index
## [1] 0.005747414

RCI with riverconn

RCI with riverconn

We calculate the REACH Connectivity Index and then play around with the function parameters.

  • We set B_ij_flag = FALSE because we do not have a dispersal kernel in this example.
  • We set index_type = "reach" because we are interested in calculating the \(RCI\).
  • We set index_mode = "to", as we consider all the connections exiting the reach of interest and going TO the network (a bit counter-intuitive…).
g <- river_graph
index <- index_calculation(graph = g, 
                           index_type = "reach", 
                           index_mode = "to",
                           weight = "length", 
                           B_ij_flag = FALSE)

RCI with riverconn

The output of the function is a data.frame with the index value for each reach. We filter the output to show the index value for the outlet.

head(index)
##   name       num      den       index
## 1    1 21.608798 813.1463 0.026574305
## 2    2 19.473934 813.1463 0.023948869
## 3    3 19.473934 813.1463 0.023948869
## 4    4  1.840274 813.1463 0.002263152
## 5    5 21.608798 813.1463 0.026574305
## 6    6 21.608798 813.1463 0.026574305
index %>% dplyr::filter(name == outlet) %>% pull(index)
## [1] 0.04734412

RCI with riverconn

We calculate the same index with asymmetric dispersal.

  • The index is higher with pass_u = 0.5 and lower with pass_u = 0.1
E(g)$pass_u <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0.5,NA)
index_calculation(g, dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric",
                  index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", B_ij_flag = FALSE) %>% 
  dplyr::filter(name == outlet) %>% pull(index)
## [1] 0.1851834
E(g)$pass_u <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0.1,NA)
index_calculation(g,  dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric",
                  index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", B_ij_flag = FALSE) %>% 
  dplyr::filter(name == outlet) %>% pull(index)
## [1] 0.05122476

RCI with riverconn

We edit the downstream passability.

  • no change: all dispersal routes are moving upstream.
E(g)$pass_d <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0.5,NA)
index_calculation(g, dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric",
                  index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", B_ij_flag = FALSE) %>% 
  dplyr::filter(name == outlet) %>% pull(index)
## [1] 0.05122476
E(g)$pass_d <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0.1,NA)
index_calculation(g,  dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric",
                  index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", B_ij_flag = FALSE) %>% 
  dplyr::filter(name == outlet) %>% pull(index)
## [1] 0.05122476

Spatial patterns of RCI

Spatial patterns of RCI

Now we analyze the spatial patterns of RCI. The index can easily be plotted together with the river shapefile. We calculate and plot the RCI with pass_u = pass_d = 0.5 and no dispersal limitation.

index_list <- list()

E(g)$pass_u <- E(g)$pass_d  <- ifelse(!is.na(E(g)$id_barrier),0.5,NA)
index <- index_list[[1]]  <- index_calculation(
    g, dir_fragmentation_type ="symmetric",
    index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", B_ij_flag = FALSE) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate("def" = "no disp, pass = 0.5")

plot <- river_net_simplified %>% 
  left_join(index, by = "name") %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(aes(color = index), size = 1) + 
  scale_color_viridis() + theme_bw() + 
  labs(color = "RCI")

Spatial patterns of RCI

Spatial patterns of RCI

We calculate and plot the RCI with pass_u = 0.1, pass_d = 0.8 and no dispersal limitation.

g <- river_graph
index <- index_list[[2]]  <- index_calculation(
    g, dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric",
    index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", B_ij_flag = FALSE) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate("def" = "no disp, pass_u=0.1, pass_d=0.9")

plot <- river_net_simplified %>% 
  left_join(index, by = "name") %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(aes(color = index), size = 1) + 
  scale_color_viridis() + theme_bw() + 
  labs(color = "RCI")

Spatial patterns of RCI

Spatial patterns of RCI

We calculate and plot the RCI with pass_u = 0.1, pass_d = 0.8 and symmetric dispersal limitation (PD = 0.5).

index <- index_list[[3]]  <- index_calculation(
    g, dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric",
    index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", param = 0.5) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate("def" = "PD=0.5, pass_u=0.1, pass_d=0.9")

plot <- river_net_simplified %>% 
  left_join(index, by = "name") %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(aes(color = index), size = 1) + 
  scale_color_viridis() + theme_bw() + 
  labs(color = "RCI")

Spatial patterns of RCI

Spatial patterns of RCI

We calculate and plot the RCI with pass_u = 0.1, pass_d = 0.8 and symmetric dispersal limitation (PD = 0.8).

index <- index_list[[4]]  <- index_calculation(
    g, dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric",
    index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", param = 0.8) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate("def" = "PD=0.8")

plot <- river_net_simplified %>% 
  left_join(index, by = "name") %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(aes(color = index), size = 1) + 
  scale_color_viridis() + theme_bw() + 
  labs(color = "RCI")

Spatial patterns of RCI

Spatial patterns of RCI

We calculate and plot the RCI with pass_u = 0.1, pass_d = 0.8 and asymmetric dispersal limitation (PDd = 0.8, PDu = 0).

index <- index_list[[5]]  <- index_calculation(
    g, dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric", dir_distance_type = "asymmetric",
    index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", param_d = 0.8, param_u = 0) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate("def" = "PDu=0, PDd=0.8")

plot <- river_net_simplified %>% 
  left_join(index, by = "name") %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(aes(color = index), size = 1) + 
  scale_color_viridis() + theme_bw() + 
  labs(color = "RCI")

Spatial patterns of RCI

Spatial patterns of RCI

We calculate and plot the RCI with pass_u = 0.1, pass_d = 0.8 and asymmetric dispersal limitation (PDd = 0.5, PDu = 0).

index <- index_list[[6]]  <- index_calculation(
    g, dir_fragmentation_type ="asymmetric", dir_distance_type = "asymmetric",
    index_type = "reach", index_mode = "to", param_d = 0.5, param_u = 0) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate("def" = "PDu=0.5, PDd=0")

plot <- river_net_simplified %>% 
  left_join(index, by = "name") %>% 
  ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(aes(color = index), size = 1) + 
  scale_color_viridis() + theme_bw() + 
  labs(color = "RCI")

Spatial patterns of RCI

Spatial patterns of RCI

We plot everything together to check the differences.

index_all <- do.call(rbind, index_list) %>% 
  group_by(def) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(rank = rank(index))

plot <- river_net_simplified %>% 
  left_join(index_all, by = "name") %>% 
  ggplot() + theme_minimal() +
  geom_sf(aes(color = rank), size = 1) + 
  scale_color_viridis() + theme_bw() + 
  labs(color = "RCI") + facet_wrap(~def) + 
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank())

Spatial patterns of RCI

Thank you for listening!
